structure.title: Fire-Resistant Plants Common to Marin County, CA

nodes.title: FIRE-RESISTANT PLANT CHARACTERISTICS
nodes.summary: This document provides a comprehensive guide to fire-resistant and fire-prone plants, specifically tailored for landscaping in Marin County, CA, to mitigate wildfire risk. It outlines the key characteristics of plants that resist ignition, such as supple, moist leaves and low deadwood, and presents an extensive list of recommended fire-resistant species categorized by their growth form (e.g., tree, shrub, perennial, groundcover, hedge, vine, grass). Conversely, the document details the characteristics of fire-prone plants, which often contain volatile oils, have fine or dry materials, and ignite easily, along with a list of species to avoid or aggressively maintain near structures. It emphasizes that no plant is "fireproof" and highlights the importance of proper plant selection, placement, spacing, maintenance, and adherence to local fire codes for creating defensible space around homes.

structure.summary: This partial document provides a comprehensive list of fire-resistant plants common to Marin County, California, detailing their scientific names, common names, and growth forms (e.g., tree, shrub, perennial, groundcover). It also outlines the key characteristics of fire-resistant plants, such as having supple, moist leaves, clean growth habits, and water-like sap. The document emphasizes that while these plants help resist fire spread, they are not fireproof and require regular irrigation and maintenance. It serves as a guide for fire-resistant landscaping and directs readers to further resources on wildfire preparedness and defensible space.

structure.title: Fire-Prone Plants

nodes.title: FIRE-PRONE PLANT CHARACTERISTICS
nodes.summary: The partial document provides lists of various plant species, including their scientific and common names, along with a single-letter code likely indicating their form or type. A significant portion of the document focuses on "fire-prone plants," detailing their characteristics (e.g., blade-leaf evergreens, volatile oils, gummy sap, fine dry material, papery bark) that make them susceptible to igniting readily and burning intensely. It advises on the removal or aggressive maintenance of these plants, especially near homes and structures, citing local fire codes. The document also includes a specific list of common fire-prone plant species found in Marin, discusses the management of fire-prone native plants, and provides a resource for further information on wildfire preparedness and defensible space.
